What you have just witnessed—the brilliant clothing, the appearance of Moses and Elijah, the voice from the cloud declaring Jesus as God's beloved Son—is not meant to settle you into comfort. The transfiguration happens, and then it ends. Jesus leads Peter, James, and John back down the mountain with a command: tell no one until the Son of Man rises from the dead. The disciples don't even understand what rising from the dead means. They're clinging to a vision of glory while their Teacher speaks of death, and they cannot reconcile the two. This is the central tension of Mark 9: you have seen Jesus transformed in radiant power, but you must now watch Him walk toward rejection and execution. The kingdom of God does not arrive the way you expect. Even the sign of His coming—Elijah's appearance—has already happened in John the Baptist, and John was beheaded. If this is how God's messenger is treated, what hope is there for the Son of Man Himself?

The moment Jesus returns to the crowd, a father rushes forward with a son tormented by an unclean spirit. The disciples have already failed to cast it out. The father's words—"if You can do anything"—reveal the fracture in his hope. Jesus doesn't offer reassurance; He offers a sharp correction: "All things are possible to him who believes." Notice the father's raw, honest response: "I do believe; help my unbelief!" This is not a plea for more faith. It is a man caught between what he desperately wants to trust and what his experience has taught him to doubt. Jesus takes him as He is—faith and unbelief mingled together—and acts. The spirit is driven out, the boy is raised up, and the power displayed on the mountaintop proves real in the valley. Yet the disciples, who saw this miracle, still do not understand what Jesus means about His death.

What follows is relentless. Jesus repeats His prediction: the Son of Man will be delivered into men's hands, killed, and raised on the third day. The disciples don't understand and are afraid to ask. Instead, they argue about who is greatest among them. Jesus sits down and teaches them something that inverts every human hierarchy: the way up is down. To be first, you must be last. To be greatest, you must serve everyone. He places a child in their midst—someone with no status, no power, no standing—and tells them that welcoming such a child in His name is welcoming Him and the Father who sent Him. Then Jesus addresses their sectarianism: someone outside their group is driving out demons in His name, and they tried to stop him. Jesus says no—whoever performs a miracle in His name cannot turn around and speak evil of Him. Yet immediately after this openness comes a warning of terrifying weight: whoever causes one of these little ones who believe in Him to stumble would be better off drowned in the sea. And then the language becomes apocalyptic and severe. If your hand causes you to sin, cut it off. If your foot causes you to sin, cut it off. If your eye causes you to sin, pluck it out. It is better to enter the kingdom of God maimed than to go to hell, where the worm never dies and the fire is never quenched.

This is not metaphorical softness. Jesus is speaking about the seriousness of sin and the reality of judgment. The kingdom of God is worth any sacrifice. Your comfort is not worth your soul. Your reputation is not worth compromise. Your desires are not worth your eternal life. Mark presents no easy path forward. You have seen the glory of Jesus transfigured. You have witnessed His power over demons. You have heard His prediction of His death and resurrection. You know His command: be last, serve others, guard the little ones, cut away whatever separates you from Him. The question is not whether you believe in Jesus—Mark assumes you do. The question is whether you will follow where He leads, down from the mountain toward a cross, and whether you will trust that His death and resurrection accomplish what He says. Everything else—your ambitions, your comfort, your need to be understood—must be salted with fire.
